speaker: interviewer
text: With Eli Benyacar. This is tape two. I'd like to go back for a moment, Mr. Benyacar, and ask you how the family survived? When the Germans came in 1941, they took your father's business. How did the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you before, that we had a lot of paper money, but it became useless. You know, the paper money became like you-- instead of it being 10 drachmas a loaf of bread, it was 10,000 drachmas a loaf of bread. So we were in bad shape. And then I started going into the black market business, you know? I'd buy cigarettes and sell them in the market, the black market. And then I used to go to the Union Station where the new German soldiers used to arrive. I used to trade cigarettes with different-- they'd give me a lighter or cheese-- what do you call them, those cheese cubes?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I used to do quite a bit with them. 12:00 noon used to be the train in, you know? This I used to speak a few words of German, and I used to get all-- they used to like Greek cigarettes. They used to have the most worst cigarettes to us, [? Extra ?] [? Palock, ?] which was the cheapest. But it was very extra, which the Germans didn't know, so I used to change with them. And that continued for a few months, I was doing this kind of business. And then at night I used to go to different taverns and buy German marks and change them with Greek denominations, you know? Anything that I can make a buck. But towards the end, it got really, really tough. So what do we do? I start selling stuff in the house. First of all, I sold my mother's sewing machine. We got some money. And then all of a sudden, my money-- my mother had two big thick gold bracelets, really heavy, that was from her dowry when she got married. So we sold those. We got quite a bit of money, because gold was still gold. There's no paper money.

speaker: interviewee
text: We kept going for a while, but no-- money wasn't coming in. Only what I was getting during the day that I was going. Because my two brothers and my father were completely absolute nothing. No- no-- no income whatsoever. Then came to the end that I sold my oldest brother's camel hair coat, which was a fortune. At the time, nobody could afford a camel hair coat. It was a year's salary. You had to work a whole year to be able to afford-- a worker, maybe over a year, would have to work to make the money to buy a camel hair-- navy blue camel hair coat. And the same one I had my sister's, but I didn't sell my sister's. I sold my brother's. We got quite a bit of money down there. And then the money was also gone. I sold-- we had, at home, we had some basins made of copper. Big, big round basins which we used to wash, make the wash on Monday to wash the clothes. Monday was washing day. Before the war my mother use to have a woman to come and help her, you know, like a maid for Monday. But I sold those too. We were coming to the end that I cannot sell, so then we starved. We used to get a loaf of bread, we used to divided it. Share and share alike. I used to cry. I used to leave my share. Not eat it. I used to go back again and try to hustle a few bucks here and there. That was-- we really had hard times until they started taking us to the ghetto. That was actually the way we worked. From we were well off, became very poor. Very, very poor towards the end of the-- what do you call them-- of the-- until the end. They started taking us apart. So we had a hard time. It was very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to help you? The church?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Church, no. No churches helped the Jews. No Jewish neighbor can help us, because they didn't have themselves. They were in trouble themselves. And time was going by day by day. And I used to wear the Star of David, but since I wasn't afraid. I was young. I used-- by 9:00 you had to be at home, you know? You're-- not a Jew were allowed, already, to be on the streets after 9:00. But I used to come at 12:00, doing black market at night in the cafes. And I never used to wear the Star of David, just-- you know. And nobody bothered me. I was very, very fortunate nobody bothered me. And as a matter of fact, there was one incident that happened to me that I almost got killed during the occupation. I was buying my German marks, changing for Greek money, you know. And I went to a Greek tavern. And it was across from the commandatore. A commandatore is a building where the German police-- those police that have those big metal things going around, that's the commandatore. But it was across the street. I didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a German sitting in the tavern, but I didn't-- I used to recognize all the signals that they used to have on their arms, whether they belonged to the SS, Wehrmacht, or whatever it was. But he was sitting on the chair on the wall so I didn't see what it was that-- what kind of a guy he was. And I was wearing my school-- in Greece, in school we had to wear a peak cap, like a school cap with a peak of around one. And I was wearing my school peak cap. And the German says to me, hey, come here. So I went over there. He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, what are you doing? Well, I said [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yeah. And I spoke a little bit of German [INAUDIBLE]. [NON-ENGLISH] in my hotel. And I used to go to the hotel. I wasn't afraid. I was [INAUDIBLE]. As soon as I walked out of the tavern, he tried to grab me. You know, to grab me. [NON-ENGLISH] So I will show you-- I was young. I was skinny, I was 110 pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just bent down and I started running. Running. It was dark and where it was near my school. You know, I knew this district very, very well. So he came running after me. And he didn't have a gun with him. He had a little bayonet, you know? So he takes the bayonet and he throws it to me, but I heard the noise of the bayonet. But I was running, running. But he was an old fat German guy. So there was, right into the-- I went into a street, which was near the ocean, like the seashore. And there's big buildings near the seashore in Salonika. So I run around, running around, running. Finally I go into those big buildings, and I go to the first floor. And I open the door and I got into there. There was the superintendent of the building in there. I start crying. The superintendent says, what's happened? What's happened, you know? In Greek, you know. I told him. He says, don't worry, he says. He took my hat, put it over the-- what do you call it? The bed. Gave me a glass of water, and he says, go into bed. So I got into bed, and he kept me there until about 12:00. And then he says, OK, now we'll take you home. He took me home. That was a Greek Gentile. He took me home. My mother, poor mother, was waiting, crying. Where am I? It was so late. So as soon as I got home, I went to the mezuzah, you know, and I prayed to God, thank God that I'm at my house. My mother says, what happened to you? Told her the story and then I almost came in tears, but I was very fortunate I made it. So that was the only thing that had happened to me during the occupation that my life was at stake, you know? Everything else was normal. And like everybody else suffered, I suffered, too. And that's why-- What other laws were in place? They made you wear the yellow star.

speaker: interviewee
text: The star.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The curfews. And that's about it, but there was no Jewish business anymore. All the Jewish business was already gone. Some of the Jewish businesses-- there were a couple big businesses which were Gentile and Jews, partners. They still, when they went back after the war, they got it back. They weren't that bad, the Greeks. But the Germans, they had a system. They had a system, and they wouldn't-- they wouldn't rush nothing. Everything was done because they had done this before. You see, they'd done it in Poland, and they'd done it in Ukrainian. They did all these countries that went ahead of time. And when they came to-- it was a special commando, they came to Salonika, because in Salonika 87% or 88% of the Jews of Greece. That was the main object there was to take the Jews from Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I would say, that's about the size of it that I remember in Greece that the time that we suffered. They had a lot of people died. A lot of people-- hunger, people dying in the streets, swollen legs from having no food, you know? Like a lot dead people, Jewish people died daily. A lot of people, as I was telling you before, they were coming from-- they were working, like where my brother was, inside Greece. They were coming in dead, they would bring them into the synagogue. Throw their bodies in the synagogue, on the four steps of the synagogue. And there were people who'd come and pick up the bodies. That was the beginning of the end, was that, you know. When they-- when the Greeks started coming back from the labor in Greece, I could see that was the beginning of the end. So they started gathering us into ghettos, as I said. They started taking the-- we used to have about six or seven sections of Jews, different groups-- different ghettos. Not like ghettos, I mean, they used to live together, like poor Jews, rich Jews, you know? First they started taking all the poor Jews, which is they started number 109. Those Jews were original in Baron Hirsch. There was-- Baron Hirsch was a Jewish poor section. When they started the transportation, which is in early January, I think, according to my book here. They took the people from Baron Hirsch so they didn't have to move them, you see? So then they start taking the different types, parts, of Salonika into Singrou. They made another ghetto, Singrou. But it was a high class Jewish people there at this section. They moved the people-- they started-- the Greeks were moving out and they were putting more Jews in there. So then they started taking us down there. We, or where I used to live, because, I used to live in a section what I would say 50-50, it was not all Jews. Just there in the apartment where we used to live, it was only three Jewish families. Three Jewish families and seven Gentile families. It was a story building, five-- then apartments, five on each side, see? And we were at the bottom, first floor bottom one. In the building of the then apartments there were only three Jewish families and the rest were all Gentile families. Actually, from where I used to live, I will say it was more Gentile than Jewish people, you know, living there. So it came to our time that we had to evacuate. So they put us into Singrou. When it came time to packing to go into Singrou--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever was left over, which was not much. Everything which-- I sold already most of the stuff. The good expensive stuff I sold. And we had a wagon so we could put the stuff, and they gave us an apartment. Or they gave us one room, I guess, for the whole family or something like that. If I remember correct. And we would have stayed there in Singrou until they make room in Baron Hirsch to put us into the trains, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bad. In Singrou, it was bad. I mean, we were living in a beautiful apartment, our home, and the building and everything. And they squashed us over there, and all. And a house with three or four families together which they put us like sardines. And then they had guards. They closed the whole section, you know? Like the whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they close it? With wires or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No just a, what do you call it? Boards. This street-- let's say it was eight streets which you can go to the ghetto. There were eight guards with those big wooden things, you know, they put in there like barriers, wooden barriers. And they had guards at every one of them. There was one German and one Greek police in every one of them. And you are in, you can't get out. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Greek police treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too bad. No, I can't tell you-- listen, I don't want to say. They weren't that bad. Some of them-- you have five fingers in your hand, they are not all the same. They're different sizes. It's all the same with the nation, and the same with the people. But most of them weren't that bad. Compared from what I hear after the war, because before the war I didn't know how other nations were treating the Jews. But from what I hear now, this we weren't that bad, treated by the Greek Jews. No, I will say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course the odd one, or a few, they want to take advantage of it, so once they leave everything down there, they grab, you know? Anybody would do that, so. But they wouldn't kill us. A Greek Gentile will not kill a Jew for the sake of taking their stuff that they own. So. How long did you stay in Singrou for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember, actually. I will say a few months. Just a couple-- two or three months. I don't remember exactly, but I do--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food? How did you manage to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we they were bringing in-- there were some stores there, but we had very little. We had very, very little food and so on. But we were-- I'll tell you the truth, I don't remember. I don't remember how we managed to do eat and these things. We must have had some food, but nothing spectacular because there were dead people in Singrou. They were dying. But I don't know how-- we had a lot of friends, like Jewish friends. Rich Jewish friends. And my brother, Soloman, had a lot of Gentile friends, as a matter of fact. He had a policeman, a couple of policemen friends, before the war. And they were helping us. They were bringing food to us, I remember. While we were in Singrou, while we were waiting, maybe they're going to take us to go to Baron Hirsch. But we didn't even know then what's going on. So they moved us there. We didn't-- no questions asked, because no news was coming out through the papers or anything like that. And then those-- we were a while is in Singrou. And all of a sudden a couple of policemen came in, into the camp, into the ghetto, friends of my brother. He says, tomorrow they are taking only the men out to Auschwitz. Or to Baron Hirsch, or whatever it is, to hard labor or something like that. So I can-- I can save you, the Greek policeman says. He says, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally he took another friend, he went through the guards there, and he sneaked us out, out of the ghetto. And we went to a Gentile home. North, I would say just an hour drive or half an hour drive. Now who were you with at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my two brothers. My father and my two sisters, they stayed. And my mother, they stayed in Singrou. Because they said they're only going to take the men. Figured well, they passed us through, and we went and we slept that night in this Gentile home on the floor, you know? And it was very nice of him. In the morning, around 7:00 in the morning, the policeman comes back. He says, they're not taking the men. They're taking the whole families. So you're not going to leave two sisters, and mother and father by themselves, while two big brothers-- of course, I was young. They go living alone. So we put the Star of David back, and they made the things. And we went back into Singrou. And next day, we hired a horse and a wagon and we put all our belongings and we went to Baron Hirsch. And Baron Hirsch was a disaster. There was nothing to eat. Everything was torn down. And it was really-- it was a poor section to start off with. And there are a few transports already had left. You see, I had one, 104, 140, 191. The Jews, Greek Jews, started at 109. 109,000. So that means that quite a few transports already left from Baron Hirsch by train to Auschwitz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about Auschwitz at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did people think they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: To meet all of friends, the Polish and Hungarian friends, in Poland. We're going to live all together, nicely, happily. All the Jews. That's what the rabbi told us, the chief rabbi. Rabbi Koretz. And he says to us, you better-- people, make sure you take heavy underwear and heavy clothes, because where you're going it's very cold. It's not like the Salonika weather. So we believed it, like dummies. We believed it. If we knew what was going on in Auschwitz, or even a slight-- at least 25% of the Jews would have survived, because the partisans were there. They could have gone to the partisans in the mountains. They'd never find you. Which a lot of-- who ever went to the partisans survived. Right after the war they came down there and they got all the properties back and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: But dummies-- we didn't realize what we were doing. And we didn't know. And we got into Baron Hirsch. So Baron Hirsch was rough. People died down there. I sneaked through the gate. I went out through the barbed wire, you know, because I was young, and sneak out. And I brought in a little bit of food for the family. But we already-- we got so exhausted. I said let's get out of here. What are we doing here? You know? We already willingly-- we wanted to leave the misery, you know? It was misery in Baron Hirsch, you know? Was the worst part about Baron Hirsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing there. They were really hitting us there, you know, the Germans. There were dying people on the street. No food. Dirt, and so on. So what are we doing here? Might as well let's go to the train. So that happened. So we went on our own. There were nearly 3,000 people every time. Every transport the trains would come in, they needed 3,000 people to fill up the trains. So they were taken. [INAUDIBLE] And we went. We could have stayed another month in Baron Hirsch. Or we could have stayed another two weeks, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very short. Very short, we couldn't-- my parents couldn't take it. The standard of living that was there, they couldn't take it. So I don't believe it was very long, because we wanted to get out of there. Finally we decided to go. Like nobody forced us, you know? OK. There was about 3,000 people, let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody made himself a rucksack with some clothes, whatever they had. And we got into the trains. And they put us, I believe it's 60 people in a cattle car, you know? And before we started moving there, so the German comes and says, OK, empty your pockets. Give us all the valuables. You know. So everybody started giving their valuables. And it didn't take long. I hear the doors close from the trains, from the wagons, and it didn't take too long. In the wagons was only two little windows on each-- one on each side, you know? However it was like enough to get a breather through this thing. We were thirsty. And we-- and the kids, there were a lot of kids, a lot of old people, and so on. And one was hitting the other one to get some room to-- they were sleeping one on top of the other. You see. There was-- it was very bad. It was five days. What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in April. April 5th. And for five days--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stopped in a couple of places, and people came in with a hose, throw water through those little windows. And everybody went to grab. They never opened the doors. They never opened the doors, but some people came with a hose and they were throwing some water through the roof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people that came with the hose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different-- they were maybe Yugoslavian or maybe Greeks, you know? Because we had to-- to go to Auschwitz we had to go through a different country, to the borders. And that was the beginning of the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other facilities like on the train? Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food. Nothing. Whatever you brought with you. They never give us anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Urine and everything like that was in sight, because we never, we never got out. For five days we never got out. So if I remember correct, we never, we never got out. So we were standing. We had a few dead people in the vehicle. And we kept them there, because they wouldn't open the door to throw the bodies out. So we had them inside. A lot old people-- we were sitting one on top of the other. You imagine 60 people in a wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: With their baggage yet, because we had some bags. Everybody took some kind of a bag with them, you know? It was-- it was one of the roughest journeys that anybody could see, but I didn't feel it. I was young. You see here again, I was very young. So in a way, my two brothers were trying to be close to me and-- I'll tell the truth, I don't remember that much, because the time went fast. Those five days in the train went like a nightmare. So we're just waiting to get out of the trains. Did people talk to one another during this journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who-- everybody was crying. Everybody was crying, it was so uncomfortable in the train. Everybody's crying. And you know, well everybody was praying. We had a couple of guys, they're both praying to God to help us. And God wasn't there, so it didn't help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally we arrived in Auschwitz. And it was April 10th, according to my book here, I remember that night. So all of a sudden, everything was full of lights. Big flood lights over there. And opened the doors, everybody rouse, rouse, rouse. You know, the Germans. Leave the baggage in there, you know. Throw the baggage on the side. And [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, like get into the lines. And they said people from 16 to 45 to one man on one side, and women also from 18 to 35 on the other side. And the rest of the old and young in another group. I was not 16 then, and I went with my parents. And all of a sudden as I'm standing up on the line with my parents, I said to myself, what I'm doing here going with old people? There's my two brothers on the other line. And I ran from one line to the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a German officer called me and says, hey you! [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I said [NON-ENGLISH]. So he let me go into the line of my brother, in the group with my brother. Then we stayed there for a few hours, until everybody-- then I see the Red Cross trucks coming in and taking the old people and the young people into trucks. And I don't know what happened. You know, they took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to us, we started marching. Marching for about an hour or hours, [INAUDIBLE], marching in lines of five or six. It was full of brothers. Brothers and Polish, all dirty. The grounds were all full of mud, muddy ground. Walking and walking. And I remember saying the soldiers that we had were not even SS. They were Ukrainians and Lithuanians soldiers, which joined the Nazi movement. And he says, whatever you got, give me a watch, whatever you got, because you're not going-- you don't-- they knew where we going. But we still don't-- we still don't know where we were going. You know? So-- I'm going to go to break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: A break? OK.
